output:
# Graph-Level Anomaly Detection: Resolving the Operator-Conflated Anomaly Scalar (OCAS) with PRISM

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Graph-level anomaly detection (GLAD) aims to identify whole-graph instances that deviate sharply from the majority of a graph population. It is now a load-bearing primitive in domains where the cost of a wrong action is high: flagging toxic or off-target molecules in drug-target and protein screening, spotting deviant brain-connectivity graphs in neuro-diagnosis, and triaging fraud rings and money-laundering subgraphs in finance. Because a small number of anomalies can drive large downstream losses, the field has converged on detectors that compress an entire graph into a single anomaly score.

The dominant recipe is counterfactual plus contrastive learning. Counterfactual GLAD methods generate counterfactual graphs and pair them with a contrastive or adversarial objective - for example the motif-consistent counterfactual-with-adversarial-refinement detector MotifCAR [1] and the imbalanced counterfactual-augmentation line [2] - while the representation backbone is typically a graph contrastive learner such as GraphCL [3], InfoGraph [4], Deep Graph Infomax [5], or the adversarially-augmented AD-GCL [6]. The shared signature is a contrast s(g) = d(phi(g), phi(O(g))) between a graph embedding phi(g) and the embedding of its augmented/counterfactual view phi(O(g)) under ONE fixed operator family O. The parallel GLAD-scalar line - GLocalKD glocal distillation [7], hierarchical memory networks HimNet [8], the one-class transformation detector OCGTL "Raising the Bar" [9], the OCGIN one-class baseline [10], dual-discriminative iGAD [14], contrastive GLADC [15], and the out-of-distribution cousin GOOD-D [11] - likewise emits a single conflated value; node-level contrastive and counterfactual anomaly detectors CoLA [13] and the attributed-network counterfactual GAD method [16] show the same pattern below the graph level.

A separate evaluation-critique line warns that these scalars may be measuring the wrong thing. Zhao and Akoglu [10] showed, observationally, that classification datasets repurposed as GLAD benchmarks are confounded by trivial size/density statistics - ROC-AUC can flip depending on which class is down-sampled, a "performance flip" driven by within-class density amplified through message passing. Tang et al. [12] showed that spectral and structural bias governs how GNNs assign anomaly signal. Together [10] and [12] establish that a single GLAD score silently mixes genuine anomaly with nuisance graph statistics, but they stop at diagnosis and propose no constructive, per-graph decomposition.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

The central finding is that the counterfactual-contrastive GLAD score suffers from a specific, nameable defect we call OCAS - the Operator-Conflated Anomaly Scalar. A model fixes one augmentation/counterfactual operator family O0 and scores each graph by the contrast s_source(g) = d(phi(g), phi(O0(g))) collapsed into a single embedding distance. This scalar is (a) OPERATOR-RELATIVE: "normality" is defined only relative to the chosen O0, so the score's meaning shifts if O0 changes; and (b) AXIS-CONFLATED: a single application of O0 perturbs edges (the STRUCTURE axis) and node features (the ATTRIBUTE axis) simultaneously, while the embedding norm and augmentation strength scale with |V|, |E|, and the degree distribution (the SIZE/DENSITY axis, per [10]).

The load-bearing argument is that s_source is a non-invertible, many-to-one projection of a deviation profile of dimension at least three. The map (structure-deviation, attribute-deviation, size-deviation) -> s_source cannot be inverted from the scalar alone: the axes provably interact because O0 is one joint operator whose action mixes edge and feature perturbations into a single contrast, and because the size/density term multiplicatively rescales BOTH the structural and attribute deviations through the embedding norm. The naive remedy - run three independent axis detectors in parallel - fails twice over: message passing makes phi a coupled nonlinear map, so a "structure-only" operator still moves the embedding partly along the attribute and size directions (the ablated operators are not orthogonal in embedding space), and three independently trained scores do not re-sum to s_source, leaving no audit that the decomposition is faithful rather than a new, differently-confounded model. The practical consequence is action mis-routing: a structural anomaly (a planted fraud ring), an attribute anomaly (a forged or mislabeled feature on a protein), and a benign large/dense graph can all produce the SAME score yet demand OPPOSITE downstream responses - network investigation, document/KYC review, or no action at all.

Near-miss honesty (mandatory). We explicitly credit the strongest prior observation: Zhao and Akoglu [10] ALREADY identified the size/density confound, showing at the benchmark level and observationally that propagation-amplified density drives the performance flip; Tang et al. [12] attributed anomaly signal to spectral/structural bias. We do not claim to discover that size/density matters. Their contribution is diagnostic and per-dataset; ours is constructive and per-graph. This positioning led, by elimination, to the PRISM idea: rather than propose yet another scalar or a benchmark caveat, re-parameterize the EXISTING detector along structure/attribute/size axes, marginalize over the operator family to strip operator-relativity, and constrain the decomposition to reconstruct the source scalar exactly. The result is a refinement that is auditable (which axis is doing the work?), ablatable (turn the size adjustment on/off), and verifiable (a literal collapse unit test), rather than a new opaque model.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Counterfactual-contrastive graph-level anomaly detectors reduce each graph to a single anomaly scalar defined relative to one fixed augmentation/counterfactual operator family, conflating structural, attribute, and benign size/density causes that demand opposite downstream actions, so how can the per-graph score be resolved into operator-marginalized structure, attribute, and size/density axes that provably collapse to the original scalar under identity-default settings?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Graph-level anomaly detection is now a load-bearing primitive in domains where the cost of a wrong action is high: flagging toxic or off-target molecules in drug-target and protein screening, spotting deviant brain-connectivity graphs in neuro-diagnosis, and triaging fraud rings and money-laundering subgraphs in finance. The dominant counterfactual-contrastive recipe - counterfactual graph generation plus a contrastive/adversarial objective, e.g. MotifCAR [1], the imbalanced counterfactual-augmentation line [2], and the GCL backbones GraphCL [3], InfoGraph [4], and AD-GCL [6] - compresses every graph into a single anomaly scalar measured against ONE fixed operator family. That scalar answers "how anomalous" but never "anomalous along which axis", and because a structural anomaly, an attribute anomaly, and a benign large/dense graph can all produce the same score yet demand opposite responses, the conflation directly mis-routes the human or automated action the detector exists to trigger. [conjectured->measured]

Resolving the scalar into an operator-marginalized structure/attribute/size profile changes how the GLAD community can EVALUATE and BUILD methods. On evaluation: Zhao and Akoglu [10] already showed, observationally, that repurposed graph-classification benchmarks are confounded by trivial size/density statistics, and Tang et al. [12] showed spectral/structural bias governs GNN anomaly scoring; PRISM turns that diagnosis into a per-graph, marginalizable SIZE axis, so axis-specific benchmarks and size-controlled leaderboards become constructible rather than merely cautionary. On method design: an explicit axis decomposition with an exact collapse identity lets future detectors be audited (which axis carries the score?), ablated (size covariate on/off), and compared on a common, operator-invariant footing instead of on a score whose meaning silently depends on the chosen operator. [provable-by-construction] for the collapse; [conjectured->measured] for the downstream evaluation gains.

The practical payoff is trustworthy, action-aligned deployment. In fraud triage, a structural-axis hit (ring topology) routes to network investigation while an attribute-axis hit (forged feature) routes to KYC/document review - different teams, different evidence. In molecular and protein screening, separating an attribute anomaly from a benign size anomaly prevents discarding viable candidates for the wrong reason; in brain-network analysis, distinguishing a connectivity-structure deviation from a global size/density artifact is the difference between a signal and a scanner confound. Brain-disorder and abnormal-protein references are motivational only: PRISM is a research instrument evaluated on public benchmark and synthetic graphs, it is not a diagnostic tool, and it makes no clinical-validity or deployment claim. Axis-attribution is presented as an auditor-facing interpretability tool; we do not release per-dataset cheapest-axis evasion guidance. When community size correlates with a protected attribute, the size/density-control knob c is enabled to adjust size as a nuisance covariate rather than treat it as anomaly signal. Any real-world deployment on identifiable social/financial graphs requires privacy safeguards (differential privacy and/or aggregation).

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The deceptively simple framing - "just compute three separate scores instead of one" - fails for five concrete, compounding reasons, and PRISM is tractable only because the collapse identity and the planted-axis oracles supply the missing anchors.

First, the axes are NOT independently observable. The source operator O0 is a single joint operator: one application perturbs structure and attribute simultaneously, while the embedding norm of phi scales with |V|, |E|, and degree, so s_source(g) = d(phi(g), phi(O0(g))) is a non-invertible many-to-one projection of a profile of dimension at least three. The naive "three parallel detectors" fix breaks on two counts: (i) non-exclusivity - a structure-only operator still moves the embedding along the attribute and size directions because message passing mixes topology and features at every layer, so the ablated operators are not orthogonal; and (ii) faithfulness - independent detectors return three numbers that do not re-sum to s_source, so there is no audit that the decomposition is faithful rather than a new, differently-confounded model. PRISM's hard constraint, AGG(w0, p) == s_source exactly, is precisely what parallel scoring cannot satisfy. [provable-by-construction]

Second, operator-marginalization needs a real augmentation family plus a controlled estimator. Making the profile operator-invariant means marginalizing over a distribution q(O) of MEANINGFUL semantics-preserving operators (arbitrary noise is the P2 placebo and must collapse separation). The marginalization is a Monte-Carlo expectation over K operator draws and needs variance control, a warmup so moments stabilize, and bias correction (small-K Monte-Carlo of a ratio/norm is biased); a sloppy estimator hides the dose-response plateau and confounds attribution with sampling noise. [conjectured->measured]

Third, size adjustment must remove NUISANCE size, not SIGNAL size. Zhao-Akoglu "anomalies" are often just large/dense graphs, but in fraud a real burst IS large, so size can be genuine signal. The covariate control c must subtract only the expected, label-independent size trend learned on normal-class graphs, leaving excess size deviation as a legitimate axis - a partialling-out problem with no ground truth on which size is nuisance. [conjectured->measured]

Fourth, there is no per-graph axis ground truth on real GLAD data - real datasets carry a binary normal/anomaly label only. Identification is rescued by two anchors: planted-axis synthetic oracles (the only available per-graph "which axis fired" labels) and the collapse identity B1 (a provable equality pinning PRISM to the source at identity defaults). Fifth, GLAD is label-scarce, one-class, small-graph, and benchmark-fragile, so a new method must be shown NOT to be just another size detector. PRISM is nonetheless well-posed because of one structural fact: the source scalar is a known, deterministic function of the ablated axes at the identity defaults. That yields a provable anchor (B1 collapse), a synthetic oracle for the missing supervision, and falsifiable dose-response plus dual placebos that bound the estimator. We are not inventing a new detector and hoping it is better - we are re-parameterizing the existing one along axes, constrained to reconstruct it exactly, then reading off which axis carried the score.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

CONCRETE NOVELTY CLAIM. PRISM is, to our knowledge, the first GLAD method that outputs an operator-MARGINALIZED, AXIS-RESOLVED anomaly profile p(g) = (a_struct, a_attr, a_size) which provably COLLAPSES, bit-for-bit up to floating tolerance, to its OWN source scalar under identity-default knobs (w = w0, q = delta_{O0}, c = off, AGG = unnormalized weighted sum). It is therefore a STRICT GENERALIZATION of the counterfactual-contrastive score, not a competing black box: the source detector is the c=off, single-operator, re-summed special case of PRISM. [provable-by-construction]

POSITIONING AGAINST PRIOR ART. (a) Counterfactual graph learning for anomaly detection - MotifCAR [1], the imbalanced counterfactual-augmentation line [2], and node-level counterfactual GAD [16] - uses counterfactual graphs to improve a single detection score or regularize a generator; none decomposes that score into marginalized structure/attribute/size axes, and none marginalizes over the operator family (they fix one O). (b) Graph contrastive learning - GraphCL [3], InfoGraph [4], Deep Graph Infomax [5], AD-GCL [6], and the contrastive node-GAD method CoLA [13] - learns representations or adversarial augmentations but still emits a conflated distance/score; AD-GCL optimizes the operator adversarially rather than MARGINALIZING over a family to remove operator-relativity, which is the opposite move. (c) GLAD scalar methods - OCGIN [10], GLocalKD [7], HimNet [8], OCGTL [9], iGAD [14], GLADC [15], and the OOD cousin GOOD-D [11] - improve the scalar (one-class boundary, glocal distillation, memory, transformation learning, dual discrimination) but each remains a single conflated value with no axis attribution and no collapse identity. (d) The eval-critique line - Zhao and Akoglu [10], Tang et al. [12] - diagnoses confounds but proposes no per-graph constructive decomposition. Every cited work occupies a different cell; none occupies {operator-marginalized} x {structure/attribute/size axis-resolved profile} x {exact collapse to its own scalar}.

NEAR-MISS HONESTY (mandatory). We explicitly acknowledge the strongest near-miss: Zhao and Akoglu [10] ALREADY identified the size/density confound in GLAD - they showed, at the benchmark level and observationally, that ROC-AUC flips with which class is down-sampled and that within-class density amplified by propagation drives it. We do NOT claim to discover that size/density matters. PRISM's novelty is constructive and per-graph where [10] is diagnostic and per-dataset: (i) it turns the size/density confound into ONE explicit, marginalizable axis of a per-graph estimator with an optional covariate adjustment knob c, (ii) it ADDS co-equal structure and attribute axes via structured operator ablations, (iii) it MARGINALIZES over the augmentation/counterfactual family to strip operator-relativity, and (iv) it proves EXACT collapse to the source scalar under identity defaults - so the decomposition is a verifiable refinement, not a new opaque detector. Relative to Tang et al. [12], who attribute anomaly signal to spectral/structural bias, PRISM operationalizes that bias as a measurable axis rather than a property of a filter. The honest one-line boundary: no prior GLAD method outputs an operator-marginalized, axis-resolved anomaly profile that PROVABLY collapses to its own scalar; [10] and [12] supply the motivating observation, PRISM supplies the actionable per-graph attribution with a collapse guarantee.

FALSIFIABILITY-BACKED, NOT ASSERTED. The novelty is guarded by a triad: exact collapse (T1, [provable-by-construction]), an operator-variance dose-response plateau (T2, [conjectured->measured]), and a planted single-axis oracle where PRISM attributes the correct axis above chance while the source scalar cannot (T3, [conjectured->measured]); plus dual placebos (random orthogonal axes; entropy-matched random operators) under which the gains must vanish if they came from capacity rather than real axis/operator structure. This is what separates PRISM from a relabeled multi-head score.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD. The encoder phi is shared with the source and capacity-matched to all baselines: a GIN (primary) or GCN (secondary) message-passing backbone of 3-5 layers with sum/mean readout producing z = phi(g) in R^d (d in {64, 128}), topped by the source's own contrastive / counterfactual-hard-negative / one-class head, so PRISM inherits exactly the source scoring function s_source(g) = d(phi(g), phi(O0(g))) for distance d (cosine or L2, matching the source). Three axis-ablated operators, each a restriction of O0, define the profile p(g) = (a_struct(g), a_attr(g), a_size(g)): O_struct perturbs topology only (edge drop/add, rewiring, subgraph counterfactual; features frozen); O_attr perturbs node/edge features only (feature masking / counterfactual shift; adjacency frozen); O_size is a size/density-controlled counterfactual holding structure and attributes at the per-graph normal profile and varying only the size covariate. The operator-marginalization knob q(O) defaults to delta_{O0} (point mass on the single source operator); in general a_axis(g) = E_{O~q}[ d(phi(g), phi(O_axis(g))) ] estimated by Monte-Carlo over K draws (K in {1, 4, 8, 16, 32}) with EMA, bias-corrected (Welford) moments, a warmup of W draws, and a reported per-graph standard error. The size covariate control c defaults to off (size leaks in as in the source); when on, a_size_adj(g) = a_size_raw(g) - lambda^T x(g) regresses raw size deviation on nuisance covariates x = (|V|, |E|, mean degree, density, degree-moment) fit on NORMAL-class graphs only, so only the label-independent trend is removed. Re-aggregation uses axis weights w (default w0 = source joint weighting) and AGG (default unnormalized weighted sum), PRISM(g) = w^T p(g). The B1 COLLAPSE UNIT TEST is the first validation gate, run before any detection claim: assert max_g |AGG(w0, p(g; q=delta_{O0}, c=off)) - s_source(g)| <= tau with tau = 1e-6. This holds by construction because, with the single operator O0 and unnormalized re-sum, the three axis ablations reconstruct the original joint contrast. [provable-by-construction]

DATASETS. Real GLAD benchmarks under the standard one-class-normal protocol: TUDataset molecular/bio (PROTEINS, DD, NCI1, MUTAG, AIDS, ENZYMES) and social (IMDB-BINARY, REDDIT-BINARY, COLLAB); validity-aware splits (GOOD-D and the Zhao-Akoglu size/density-aware protocol) to test whether PRISM attributes "anomalies" to the size axis; and, where available, protein-interaction / brain functional-connectivity graphs and a financial/transaction set (size-as-genuine-signal regime). Synthetic planted-axis oracle graphs (Barabasi-Albert, Erdos-Renyi, Stochastic Block Model) provide the only per-graph axis ground truth via three single-axis injections - structure-only (planted ring/clique/motif), attribute-only (feature shift on a node subset), size-only (benign enlargement/densification) - plus a shuffled-label NULL. All reported experiments use de-identified or public benchmark graphs; no identifiable PII graphs are used.

BASELINES. The source method itself (PRISM must collapse to it, B1); deep GLAD detectors OCGIN, GLocalKD, GLADC, OCGTL, iGAD, HimNet, and GOOD-D; the Zhao-Akoglu trivial size/density/propagation-statistic detectors; a capacity-matched single-score model (to rule out "the win is just extra capacity"); and the internal placebo controls P1 (random-orthogonal-axis PRISM) and P2 (entropy-matched random-operator PRISM).

METRICS. Detection parity via graph-level AUROC/AUPRC per dataset (mean and worst-dataset). The CORE NEW metric is COLLAPSE-FIDELITY ERROR E_collapse = max_g |PRISM(g; w0, delta_{O0}, off) - s_source(g)|, target <= 1e-6, which certifies the decomposition is faithful before any claim. Additional axis metrics: per-axis attribution accuracy on planted oracles (vs chance ~1/3), operator-relative variance reduction (T2), per-domain attribution mix, and estimator standard error / K-convergence. We also report per-subgroup and worst-group detection metrics alongside aggregate scores, and PRISM axis-attribution disaggregated by subgroup, with a fairness diagnostic that checks whether the SIZE/DENSITY axis carries disparate flagging across groups. ABLATIONS cover B1 boundary recovery (tau sweep), an axis factorial cube (struct x attr x size on/off), the operator-support dose-response sweep (T2 plateau), c on/off (size-leakage study), the K-sample/variance study, the dual placebos, and the planted-oracle attribution with shuffled-label null (T3).

EXPECTED OUTCOMES. (1) B1 PASSES, E_collapse <= 1e-6 on every eval set - PRISM is exactly the source at identity defaults. [provable-by-construction] (2) PARITY + ATTRIBUTION: PRISM matches the source AUROC/AUPRC in aggregate while uniquely attributing the firing axis on planted oracles (accuracy >> 1/3), where the source scalar attributes at chance. [conjectured->measured] (3) HEADLINE: on real benchmarks a non-trivial fraction of flagged "anomalies" attribute to the SIZE axis, re-deriving the Zhao-Akoglu confound constructively and per-graph, with the structure-vs-attribute mix shifting by domain; turning c on partials out that size mass and exposes residual structure/attribute anomalies. [conjectured->measured] (4) PLACEBOS NULL OUT: P1 and P2 both destroy the gains. [conjectured->measured] (5) DOSE-RESPONSE: T2 operator-relative variance decreases monotonically to a plateau. [conjectured->measured] We flag adversarial evasion - an attacker tuning the lowest-cost axis (cf. Szegedy et al. 2014) - and scope evasion-robustness evaluation under adaptive adversaries as future work. We distinguish [provable-by-construction] claims (exact collapse to the source scalar; non-invertibility of the single scalar) from [conjectured->measured] claims (axis attribution on real data, dose-response plateau, planted-axis recovery): real-data axis isolation is empirically measured, not proven.

## 4. References and Evidence

VERIFIED (arXiv ID confirmed by direct arXiv API lookup):

[1] Xiao, C., Pang, S., Tai, W., Huang, Y., Trajcevski, G., and Zhou, F. (2024). "Motif-Consistent Counterfactuals with Adversarial Refinement for Graph-Level Anomaly Detection (MotifCAR)." ACM SIGKDD (KDD) 2024. arXiv:2407.13251.

[2] (Authors per arXiv listing.) (2024). "Imbalanced Graph-Level Anomaly Detection via Counterfactual Augmentation and Feature Learning." 2024. arXiv:2407.11082.

[3] You, Y., Chen, T., Sui, Y., Chen, T., Wang, Z., and Shen, Y. (2020). "Graph Contrastive Learning with Augmentations (GraphCL)." NeurIPS 2020. arXiv:2010.13902.

[4] Sun, F.-Y., Hoffmann, J., Verma, V., and Tang, J. (2020). "InfoGraph: Unsupervised and Semi-supervised Graph-Level Representation Learning via Mutual Information Maximization." ICLR 2020. arXiv:1908.01000.

[5] Velickovic, P., Fedus, W., Hamilton, W. L., Lio, P., Bengio, Y., and Hjelm, R. D. (2019). "Deep Graph Infomax (DGI)." ICLR 2019. arXiv:1809.10341.

[6] Suresh, S., Li, P., Hao, C., and Neville, J. (2021). "Adversarial Graph Augmentation to Improve Graph Contrastive Learning (AD-GCL)." NeurIPS 2021. arXiv:2106.05819.

[7] Ma, R., Pang, G., Chen, L., and van den Hengel, A. (2022). "Deep Graph-level Anomaly Detection by Glocal Knowledge Distillation (GLocalKD)." WSDM 2022. arXiv:2112.10063.

[8] Niu, C., Pang, G., and Chen, L. (2023). "Graph-level Anomaly Detection via Hierarchical Memory Networks (HimNet)." ECML-PKDD 2023. arXiv:2307.00755.

[9] Qiu, C., Kloft, M., Mandt, S., and Rudolph, M. (2022). "Raising the Bar in Graph-level Anomaly Detection (OCGTL)." IJCAI 2022. arXiv:2205.13845.

[10] Zhao, L. and Akoglu, L. (2023). "On Using Classification Datasets to Evaluate Graph-Level Outlier Detection: Peculiar Observations and New Insights." Big Data 11(3):151-180, 2023. arXiv:2012.12931. (Introduces OCGIN; diagnoses the size/density "performance flip" confound.)

[11] Liu, Y., Ding, K., Liu, H., and Pan, S. (2023). "GOOD-D: On Unsupervised Graph Out-Of-Distribution Detection." WSDM 2023. arXiv:2211.04208.

[12] Tang, J., Li, J., Gao, Z., and Li, J. (2022). "Rethinking Graph Neural Networks for Anomaly Detection (BWGNN)." ICML 2022. arXiv:2205.15508.

[13] Liu, Y., Li, Z., Pan, S., Gong, C., Zhou, C., and Karypis, G. (2021). "Anomaly Detection on Attributed Networks via Contrastive Self-Supervised Learning (CoLA)." IEEE TNNLS, 2021. arXiv:2103.00113.

UNVERIFIED ARXIV (real papers; venue and year given, arXiv ID NOT verified, so no ID asserted):

[14] Zhang, G., et al. (2022). "Dual-discriminative Graph Neural Network for Imbalanced Graph-level Anomaly Detection (iGAD)." NeurIPS 2022.

[15] Luo, X., et al. (2022). "Deep Graph Level Anomaly Detection with Contrastive Learning (GLADC)." Scientific Reports 12, 2022.

[16] Xiao, C., et al. (2023). "Counterfactual Graph Learning for Anomaly Detection on Attributed Networks." IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering (TKDE), 2023.

Evidence note: All 13 verified entries had arXiv IDs confirmed by direct arxiv.org API lookups; entries [14], [15], and [16] are real venues whose arXiv IDs could not be confirmed, so no ID is asserted for them. The eval-critique pair [10] and [12] is the mandatory near-miss and is always credited for the observational size/density diagnosis before any constructive per-graph claim.
